macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 114 and Inheritance as Din (Monetary Judgment)

micro_segments.title: Overview of the daf and shift from tree of inheritance to din and mishpat
Tanya Rabba bar Chinena: Inheritance may only occur during daytime, not at night
Abaye's challenge: Does the rule mean inheritance only happens if death occurs during day?
Resolution: The rule applies to adjudication of inheritance (din nachalot), not the fact of death
Baraita source: Chukkat mishpat—inheritance is designated as a form of din
Rashbam: The chiddush is that nachalot is dinei mamonot (monetary judgment)
Rambam, Rashba, and other Rishonim: Chukkat mishpat is more weighty than ordinary dinei mamonot; not subject to dina d'malchuta

macro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's Principle: Din Must Begin in Daytime; Three Visitors to Dying Person

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's rule: All din must start in daytime; dinei nefashos must both start and end in day
Case of three visitors to a dying person (shochiv mera) who hear his will or property distribution

macro_segments.title: Discussion of Matnat Shechiv Mera and Forms of Deathbed Bequest

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka: A dying person may redistribute property among his heirs (not to non-heirs)
Matnat Shechiv Mera: Verbal bequest on deathbed that becomes binding if no retraction before death
Kinyan bemiktzat: Standard kinyan (e.g., sudar) used to transfer property, effective at moment of death
Three methods of controlling inheritance: Rabbi Yochanan's redistribution, Matnat Shechiv Mera, and Kinyan Bemiktzat

macro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's Principle: Din Must Begin in Daytime; Three Visitors to Dying Person (continued)

micro_segments.title: Three options for visitors: serve as witnesses only, or function as Beit Din if three present
Rav Chisda: Three visitors can function as Beit Din only if they visited during daytime
If visited at night, even three cannot function as Beit Din; can only serve as witnesses
Reason: A Beit Din cannot open a case at night; principle of Ein Ed Na'aseh Dayan

macro_segments.title: Tosafot on Chukkat Mishpat and Inability to Select Beit Din

micro_segments.title: Tosafot: The chiddush of chukkat mishpat is that heirs cannot choose which Beit Din adjudicates
Unlike ordinary dinei mamonot, a Beit Din that witnessed the bequest may judge even against heirs' consent
Rashi's view: If designated as witness, one cannot serve as judge (Tosafot disagrees)
Distinction between Ein Ed Na'aseh Dayan: ed hame'id (one who testified) versus ed haro'eh (one who merely witnessed)

macro_segments.title: Digression: Kinyan Chalipin—When May One Retract from a Deal?

micro_segments.title: Question: How long after performing kinyan chalipin (e.g., taking a pen) may one back out?
Rabba: One may retract as long as both parties remain in the same room (kol zman sheyoshvin)
Rav Yosef: One may retract only while still discussing the same subject matter (kol zman she'asukin be'oto inyan)
Rationale: Kinyan chalipin depends entirely on mental commitment (gmirot da'at), not physical act; hence time for completion of intent
Application to dying person case: If Rav Yosef's view, they switch topics before judgment; if Rabba's view, they leave the room first
Halakha: We rule like Rav Yosef in three Bava Batra cases: sadeh, inyan, and mechtza

macro_segments.title: Return to Chain of Inheritance: The Mishna's Apparent Redundancy

micro_segments.title: Mishna: A man inherits from his mother and wife, but they do not inherit from him (and vice versa for women)
Gemara question: Why repeat the same rule in reverse? It is redundant.
Answer: The repetition teaches that a woman and her son are analogous to a woman and her husband regarding bakever (grave) inheritance
Bakever principle: One does not inherit if dead; married couple relationship terminates at death (no blood kinship to in-laws remains)
Contrast: Blood relations (son, father, brother) do inherit bakever because lineage continues; but wife and mother do not

macro_segments.title: Rav Yochanan's Radical Position: Mother Inherits from Son

micro_segments.title: Rav Yochanan in name of Rabbi Yehuda ben Rabbi Shimon: Father and mother both inherit from son (Torah law)
Source: Daughters of Zelophehad—if a daughter can inherit, so can a mother (she'erus parallel to father's tribe)
Rashbam: This rule applies only if father is not alive; if father exists, he inherits
Tosafot's objection: The view is disruptive and would undermine patriarchal family structure and prevent property reaching father's brothers
Tosafot's reinterpretation: The rule applies only in the case of a ger (convert) with no other relatives, to prevent property becoming hefker
Assessment: Rav Yochanan's position is radical, applying Daughters of Zelophehad equality principle to mothers as well as daughters
